data.title: Anheuser-Busch's new energy drink partnership hits 7-Eleven stores
data.description:
Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) has introduced a new energy drink called Phorm Energy into 7-Eleven stores across the United States this month. The rollout follows an earlier online-only debut. Anheuser-Busch (BUD) is making its aggressive move into the energy drink category in partnership with St. Louis-based 1st Phorm and UFC President Dana White.

Phorm Energy comes in four flavors: Blue Blitz, Orange Fury, Grape Smash, and Screamin' Freedom. Each 16-ounce can features 200mg of caffeine from green tea extract, an electrolyte blend for hydration, and contains zero sugar and no artificial flavors. The product is designed for consumers seeking both physical and mental energy and is positioned as a drink for those "who show up and do the work" every day. A promotion runs through the end of the year, offering two cans for $5 exclusively at participating 7-Eleven stores for customers aged 18 and up.

 

 

 

The keyword Phorm Energy saw a notable spike of Google search interest in June and is still trending strong.

data.title: Gabriel Resources announces C$3.6M units offering
data.description:
• Gabriel Resources (TSXV:GBU:CA) is offering up to 34,305,000 units at C$0.105 each to raise gross proceeds of ~C$3.6 million (US$2.625 million).
  • Each unit will consist of one common share in the capital of the company and one common share purchase warrant. Each warrant will entitle the holder to purchase one share for a period of five years from the date of issuance at an exercise price of C$0.14 per share.
  • The net proceeds of the offering are intended to be used for general corporate purposes.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Lotus Technology reports 1H results
data.description:
• Lotus Technology press release (NASDAQ:LOT): 1H net loss was $313 million for the first half of 2025, narrowed by 32% YoY.
  • Revenue of $218M (+45% Y/Y)
  • Gross margin for the first half was 8.2%, versus 12.8% for the first half of 2024.
  • Operating loss was $263 million for the first half of 2025, narrowed by 40% YoY.

data.title: Alibaba Non-GAAP EPADS of $2.02 misses by $0.14, revenue of $34.57B misses by $910M
data.description:
• Alibaba press release (NYSE:BABA): Q1 Non-GAAP EPADS of $2.02 misses by $0.14.
  • Revenue of $34.57B (+2% Y/Y) misses by $910M.
  • Revenue was RMB247,652 million (US$34,571 million), an increase of 2% year-over-year. Excluding revenue from the disposed businesses of Sun Art and Intime, revenue on a like-for-like basis would have grown by 10% year-over-year.

data.title: U.S. sends warships near Venezuela, escalating tensions
data.description:
The U.S. is expanding it naval presence in the Southern Caribbean near Venezuela's territorial waters, with the aim of addressing threats from Latin American drug cartels.

Seven U.S. warships and a nuclear-powered fast attack submarine are either in the area or are expected to arrive in the coming week, a U.S. official told Reuters. This buildup is significantly bigger than usual U.S. deployments in the region.

The Pentagon has not yet disclosed what the mission will be, but Secretary of State Marco Rubio previously said that the U.S. government could use the military to go after Latin American drug cartels.

Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro denounced the naval buildup, saying there's "no way" that the U.S. can enter Venezuela. "Today, we are more prepared to defend peace, sovereignty and territorial integrity."

Venezuela announced the deployment of 15,000 troops to its border with Colombia. Its government also called on citizens to join a volunteer militia to assist armed forces in response to the threat of a U.S. invasion. 

"It's a massive propaganda operation to justify what the experts call kinetic action - meaning military intervention in a country which is a sovereign and independent country and is no threat to anyone," Samuel Moncada, Venezuela's UN ambassador, said. "… it's ridiculous to think that they're fighting drug trafficking with nuclear submarines."

The U.S. is offering a $50M reward for information that would lead to Maduro's arrest. "Maduro helped manage and ultimately lead the Cartel of the Suns, a Venezuelan drug-trafficking organization comprised of high-ranking Venezuelan officials," the State Department has alleged. Washington designated the cartel as a terrorist group.
